name: Common Ground Award
summary: Capital grants of up to £10,000 (or up to £50,000 for exceptional scale-up) for VCSE organisations in England delivering projects that bridge social capital across communities.

eligibility.label: Organisation must be a VCSE body (charity, CIO, CIC, CBS, social enterprise, or faith-based organisation)
Organisation has a base in and operates in England
Organisation exists for charitable, benevolent, or philanthropic purposes
Organisation is not a local authority, public sector body, profit-distributing entity, or education sector organisation

criteria.label: Strategic alignment — alignment with the fund's bridging social capital objective

guidance.what_we_look_for: Evidence that the project aligns with the fund's objective of bridging social capital — bringing together people from different backgrounds, communities, or organisations to build trust, understanding, and shared purpose.

scores.0: No evidence of alignment with the bridging social capital objective. Project appears focused on bonding capital within a single community or has no clear social capital aim.
scores.1: Some reference to bridging social capital but the link between project activities and the fund objective is weak or superficial.
scores.2: Clear alignment with the bridging social capital objective. The project is designed to bring different groups together with a plausible theory of change.
scores.3: Exceptional alignment. The project demonstrates deep understanding of bridging social capital, with activities directly designed to connect diverse communities and a compelling rationale for how this will be achieved.

criteria.label: Community engagement — engagement with communities and organisations

guidance.what_we_look_for: Evidence that the organisation engages meaningfully with the communities it serves and partners with other organisations. This includes co-design, consultation, partnership working, and responsiveness to community needs.

scores.0: No evidence of community engagement. The project appears designed without input from the communities it intends to serve.
scores.1: Some engagement is described but it is limited, tokenistic, or not clearly linked to the project design.
scores.2: Credible community engagement. The organisation demonstrates ongoing relationships with target communities and has involved them in shaping the project.
scores.3: Outstanding engagement. The project is co-designed with diverse community members and organisations. There is clear evidence of sustained, reciprocal relationships and a track record of responsive, community-led work.

criteria.label: Community impact — demonstrable outcomes including interaction, trust, networks, and wellbeing

guidance.what_we_look_for: Clear, measurable outcomes for communities — increased interaction across groups, improved trust, stronger networks, and enhanced wellbeing. The application should describe how impact will be evidenced.

scores.0: No measurable outcomes defined, or outcomes are purely activity-based (e.g. number of events held) with no link to social capital or wellbeing.
scores.1: Some outcomes are stated but they are vague, unmeasurable, or not clearly linked to bridging social capital.
scores.2: Clear outcomes with appropriate indicators. The application explains how increased interaction, trust, networks, or wellbeing will be measured.
scores.3: Ambitious, specific, and measurable outcomes across multiple dimensions of social capital and wellbeing. Includes a credible approach to evidence collection and a realistic theory of change.

criteria.label: Sustainability — longer-term plans and evaluation approach

guidance.what_we_look_for: Evidence that the project has a plan for sustainability beyond the grant period and an approach to evaluating its impact. The sustainability score also drives award size — higher scores may unlock larger awards where fund availability permits.

scores.0: No consideration of sustainability or evaluation. The project appears to end entirely when funding stops.
scores.1: Some mention of sustainability but plans are vague or unrealistic. Evaluation approach is minimal.
scores.2: Credible sustainability plan with identified funding sources or partnership commitments. A clear evaluation approach is described.
scores.3: Exceptional sustainability planning. The project is designed to become self-sustaining or to embed lasting change. Evaluation is robust, with plans to share learning. This score may support a larger award where fund availability permits.

criteria.label: Use of funding — reasonable use of capital investment

guidance.what_we_look_for: A clear and reasonable capital spending plan. Items should be appropriate for a capital grant (physical assets, equipment, refurbishment — not staffing or running costs). Costs should be realistic and represent value for money.

scores.0: No spending plan provided, or the proposed items are not capital expenditure (e.g. staff costs, revenue activities).
scores.1: A spending plan is provided but items are poorly justified, costs seem inflated, or the link between capital items and project outcomes is unclear.
scores.2: Clear capital spending plan with reasonable costs. Items are appropriate and linked to project delivery.
scores.3: Excellent value for money. Every item is justified with evidence (e.g. quotes, benchmarks). The capital investment clearly enables the bridging social capital outcomes described elsewhere in the application.

award_ranges.notes: Standard awards up to £10,000. Exceptional scale-up awards up to £50,000 based on high sustainability score and fund availability.
